'''Rangitāwhana Chase''' (16 November 1918 – 7 September 1998) was a [[rugby league]] player. He represented the [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand rugby league team]] in 10 matches from 1937 to 1938. In the process he became the 248th player to represent New Zealand. He also represented [[New Zealand Māori rugby league team|New Zealand Māori]] against [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]], as well as the [[North Island]] and [[Auckland rugby league team|Auckland]]. He played his club rugby league in [[Auckland]] for the [[Manukau Magpies|Manukau]] side. He also represented [[Whanganui Rugby Football Union|Whanganui]] in rugby union and played for the Huia club, as well as making representative appearances for several sub union sides.

==Early life==
Rangitāwhana Chase was born on 16 November 1918 to Henare (Harry) Chase (1887–1949), and Erina Tamara Weatherley (1894–1957) in [[Moawhango]], in rural [[Whanganui]]. They had several children together. He had a sister, Ngahua o te Maramatanga (Ginny) Chase (1922–2005), born in Taihape, and another sister Peggy Te Miringa Chase Whenuaroa (1925–2002). Another sister, Kewa Ruby Chase was also born around this time with no fixed date of birth. She died in 1977. Henare also had many children to Miriama Te Pirihi Kaihote.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Henare Harry Chase
| publisher = Family Search
| url =https://ancestors.familysearch.org/en/LB2Q-C5Y/henare-harry-chase-1881-1949
| access-date = 26 July 2022}}</ref> Rangi's older half brother [[Tommy Chase|Tame Thomas (Tommy) Chase]] also played representative rugby union for [[Whanganui Rugby Football Union|Whanganui]] and moved to [[Auckland]] with Rangi to play rugby league for [[Manukau Magpies|Manukau]] in [[1938 Auckland Rugby League season|1938]]. He also represented [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] at rugby league in 1939.

==Playing career==
===Whanganui rugby===
Rangi Chase played his early rugby for the Huia club in the [[Taihape]] area, located in the [[Whanganui]] province. They were a team dominated by local [[Māori people|Māori]] players. In 1933, aged 14 he played for Huia against Pirates on 17 June and scored a try. In 1998 these two clubs merged to form the Taihape Rugby & Sports Club.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Junior Football
| page = 10
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 76, Issue 143
| date = 20 June 1933
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19330620.2.116
| access-date = 26 July 2022}}</ref> In 1934 Chase was selected for the Taihape sub-union representative side for their match with [[Rangitikei District|Rangitikei]] on 11 August whilst still aged 15. The match was played at [[Mangaweka]] and Taihape won 14 to 5 with Chase scoring 2 tries. It was reported in the [[Whanganui Chronicle]] that Chase had been one of their "best men". With the score at 5–0 he "broke away for Taihape, and with the ball at his toe carried the play into midfield", a while later from a line-out "the ball went to Graham who sent it over to Chase, the latter scoring a try". Soon after he received a pass from Gregory to cross for his second try.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Junior Rugby/Taihape Defeats Rangitikei/Representative Teams Meet At Mangawaka
| page = 4
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 77, Issue 190
| date = 13 August 1934
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19340813.2.8.7
| access-date = 26 July 2022}}</ref>

The 1935 season started with Chase still playing in Huia's senior side against Hautapu on 25 May despite still being regarded as a junior and aged just 16. It was reported that "he certainly did not let his side down, as he was very sound at full-back, his fielding and line-kicking being excellent" in an 8–6 win.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Taihape/Rugby Football/Huia Defeat Hautapu
| page = 3
| publisher =Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 124
| date = 28 May 1935
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19350528.2.8.2
| access-date = 26 July 2022}}</ref> On the same day in an earlier junior match he played for the Huia junior side and scored a try and kicked a penalty in a 14–9 win over Utiku.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Junior Football
| page = 3
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 127
| date = 31 May 1935
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19350531.2.8.5
| access-date = 26 July 2022}}</ref> The following weekend he played for the seniors once more in a 9–9 draw with Utiku on the number 2 ground at [[Taihape]]. It was reported that he (along with Burrell of Utiku) "gave good exhibitions as the last lines of defence, their handling being clear, while [Chase] came to light with some really excellent line kicks".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Taihape Rugby/Huia And Utiku/Interesting Drawn Game
| page = 3
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 131
| date = 5 June 1925
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19350605.2.8
| access-date = 26 July 2022}}</ref> A few weeks later Chase was selected at full back for the Taihape Māori side to play Rangitikei Māori on 3 July for the Whenuaroa Shield.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Maori Rugby/Taihape v. Rangitikei
| page = 3
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 153
| date = 3 July 1925
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19350703.2.6.1
| access-date = 26 July 2022}}</ref> Taihape Māori lost 22–14 with the only mention of Chase as missing a conversion and then missing a chance to force the ball allowing a try to be scored by his opponent.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Maori Rugby/Whenuaroa Shield/Rangitikei Lifts It/Defeat Of Taihape
| page = 5
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 155
| date = 5 July 1935
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19350705.2.25
| access-date = 26 July 2022}}</ref> The only mention of Chase in the newspapers over the remainder of the season was a Taihape junior match against Rangitikei juniors on 20 July at Taihape. Chase was selected to play on the wing. Taihape won the match 11–3.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Taihape/Rugby Football/Taihape v. Rangitikei
| page = 9
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 164
| date = 16 July 1935
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19350716.2.102
| access-date = 26 July 2022}}</ref>

By 1936 Chase was well established in the Huia side and played at fullback in their early season match with Utiki on 16 May. Utiku won 9 to 3, with Rangi's older brother [[Tommy Chase]] also playing but he (Tommy) "failed to reveal his true form".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Utiki v. Huia
| page = 3
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 117
| date = 19 May 1936
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19360519.2.5.4
| access-date = 26 July 2022}}</ref> On 13 June Rangi scored 2 tries for Huia against [[Mangaweka]] at [[Taihape]] in a 21–21 loss. His brother Tommy missed some easy conversions but "nevertheless played an excellent game as did also his brother, Rangi Chase, who was outstanding and scored two fine tries". He had another disallowed for a forward pass. His first try came after the backs got "in motion and he made a determined run which carried him over". His second came when "Payne, Raukawa, T. Chase, and R. Chase handled a bright movement culminating in a try for R. Chase" however he failed to convert it and the chance to win the match was missed.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Rugby Football/Mangaweka v. Huia
| page = 3
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 141
| date = 16 June 1936
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19360616.2.8.3
| access-date = 26 July 2022}}</ref>

On the morning of 24 June a personal note appeared in the [[Whanganui Chronicle]] stating that "Messrs. Tommy and Rangi Chase, J. Raukawa, and Wereta, left Taihape yesterday morning for [[Waitara, New Zealand|Waitara]] to play for the Whanganui Māori rugby team against Taranaki Māori [team] for the Tuera Shield."<ref>{{cite news
| title =Personal
| page = 3
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 149
| date = 25 June 1936
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19360625.2.9
| access-date = 26 July 2022}}</ref> The Tuera Shield is still played for today after being first played for in 1896.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Taranaki Maori claim 124 year old Tuera Shield from Whanganui Maori at Cooks Gardens
| publisher = Whanganui Rugby Football Union
| date = 3 October 2020
| url =https://wanganuirugby.co.nz/wp/2020/10/03/taranaki-maori-claim-124-year-old-tuera-shield-from-whanganui-maori-at-cooks-gardens/
| access-date = 27 July 2022}}</ref> Taranaki Māori won by 18 to 9 at Waitara.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Waitara Hui/Taranaki Rugbyites Beat Wanganui
| page = 4
| publisher = Gisborne Times, Volume LXXXIV, Issue 12898
| date = 26 June 1936
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/GIST19360626.2.36
| access-date = 27 July 2022}}</ref> On 1 July Chase played for Taihape against Rangitikei. He was at five eighth in the place of Tommy, who was injured in the Whanganui Māori match, and scored 2 tries in a 27–6 win. Rangi "was outstanding, especially on attack, and scored two excellent tries", he "was the star player in the Taihape rearguard and will doubtless retain his place in the Taihape representative team".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Taihape Rugby/Defeat Of Rangitikei
| page = 4
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 156
| date = 3 July 1936
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19360703.2.17
| access-date = 27 July 2022}}</ref> A week later he scored another try for Huia in a 8–6 win over Old Boys. Chase then played for Taihape at five eighth against Whanganui Metropolitan on 18 July and scored a try once more. The match was for the Pownall Trophy and his brother Tommy was in the three-quarters.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Huia v. Old Boys
| page = 3
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 165
| date = 14 July 1936
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19360714.2.6.3
| access-date = 27 July 2022}}</ref> Whanganui won the match 34–14 at Spriggens Park to retain the trophy. It was said that of the Taihape backs "Chase was undoubtedly the best. In fact he was the only back showing representative form… he made several tricky openings for Taihape, but lack of support of the right type brought most of them to failure". His try came "as the result of a quick stab-kick and a clever gather in".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Pownall Trophy/Retained By Wanganui/Taihape Team Defeated
| page = 4
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 170
| date = 20 July 1936
| url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19360720.2.14
| access-date = 27 July 2022}}</ref>

===Whanganui rugby selection===
Prior to the match between [[Taihape]] and [[Whanganui]] Metropolitan, Chase had been selected in a 25 player squad for the Whanganui representative side which was asked to "keep fit".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Wanganui Rugby/25 Players To Keep Fit
| page = 4
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 167
| date = 16 July 1936
| url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19360716.2.11.3
| access-date = 27 July 2022}}</ref> His performance in the match which was essentially a trial was good enough for him to be selected at second five eighth for Whanganui's match with [[Waikato Rugby Union|Waikato]] 3 days later on 21 July.<ref>{{cite news
| title = To-morrow's Match/Waikato v. Wanganui
| page = 4
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 170
| date = 20 July 1936
| url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19360720.2.15
| access-date = 27 July 2022}}</ref> Whanganui won 17 to 9 with Chase scoring a try after "strong running by Barton and a flash through by Chase".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Win For Wanganui/Representative Rugby
| page = 12
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 172
| date = 22 July 1936
| url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19360722.2.105
| access-date = 27 July 2022}}</ref> The Whanganui Chronicle after the match said that "Barton played a sound game at centre, and was the best Whanganui back. Thompson, at first five eight ranked next, with Chase close behind him". They went on the say that "R. Chase was the one star in the [Taihape] maroon backs on Saturday, and the fact that he played so well for a losing team justified the selectors in placing him in the representative side for yesterday's match".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Rugby In Review/Comment On Play And Players
| page = 4
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 172
| date = 22 July 1936
| url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19360722.2.10
| access-date = 27 July 2022}}</ref> Chase then played at five-eighths for Taihape against Rangitikei on 8 August at Hunterville Domain in [[Rangitikei District|Rangitikei]].<ref>{{cite news
| title = Rugby Football/Rangitikei v. Taihape
| page = 3
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 183
| date = 4 August 1936
| url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19360804.2.9
| access-date = 27 July 2022}}</ref> Taihape lost 9–0 with Chase said to have been "sound".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Representative Rugby/Rangitikei Outclass Taihape
| page = 4
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 189
| date = 11 August 1936
| url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19360811.2.10
| access-date = 27 July 2022}}</ref>

Chase was then chosen to make his second appearance for Whanganui against [[Bush Rugby Football Union|Bush Union]] at [[Pahiatua]] on 12 August.<ref>{{cite news
| title = To Play Bush Union/Wanganui's Challenge To-Day
| page = 12
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 190
| date = 12 August 1936
| url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19360812.2.102
| access-date = 27 July 2022}}</ref> Bush won 9–5 with Chase scoring Whanganui's only try.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Win For Bush Team/Match With Wanganui
| page = 12
| publisher = Manawatu Standard, Volume LVI, Issue 217
| date = 13 August 1936
| url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/MS19360813.2.130
| access-date = 27 July 2022}}</ref> The weather was poor with it raining hard and a gale-force wind. His try came with ten minutes left in the match on a "well ploughed up" ground.
<ref>{{cite news
| title = Representative Rugby/Bush Beat Wanganui
| page = 2
| publisher = Manawatu Times, Volume 61, Issue 190
| date = 13 August 1936
| url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/MT19360813.2.4
| access-date = 27 July 2022}}</ref> He had begun the game at second five-eighth and was involved in several attacking movements and came close to scoring more than once. During the first half Barton moved into second five and Chase moved out to centre. Late in the match a kick went behind the Bush side and "Chase tore into the opening and made a dive at the ball just as H. Wolland went back to force, the referee awarding the Wanganui centre a try".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Muddy Rugby/Bush Again Triumphant
| page = 5
| publisher = Pahiatua Herald, Volume XLIII, Issue 13333
| date = 13 August 1936
| url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/PAHH19360813.2.19
| access-date = 27 July 2022}}</ref> He was named to play for Taihape against [[Taumarunui]] but did not play in the match. Chase was then named in the Whanganui side to play [[Taranaki Rugby Football Union|Taranaki]] at [[New Plymouth]] at second five-eighth.<ref>{{cite news
| title = To Play Taranaki/Wanganui Teams
| page =4
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 193
| date = 17 August 1936
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19360817.2.15
| access-date = 28 July 2022}}</ref> Whanganui lost 13–0 but Chase was said to have played well.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Forwards' Struggle/Game At New Plymouth
| page = 4
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 199
| date = 24 August 1936
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19360824.2.8.11
| access-date = 28 July 2022}}</ref>

Chase was then chosen in the five-eighths with D. Thompson in the Whanganui team to play the touring [[Australia national rugby union team|Australian]] side on 26 August.<ref>{{cite news
| title = With The Colleges/Nelson The Champions/Defeat Of Wellington
| page = 4
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 200
| date = 25 August 1936
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19360825.2.12
| access-date = 28 July 2022}}</ref> The team was supposed to assemble for a practice and team meeting the evening before the game. However Chase "was relegated to the sideline. He failed to muster with the other players for practice prior to the match against the Australians and the "Big Three" stood him down and called on Dave Jones (Kaierau)".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Rugby Comment/Jottings About The Game
| page = 4
| publisher =Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 207
| date = 2 September 1936
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19360902.2.14
| access-date = 28 July 2022}}</ref> He played 2 further matches in the season. The first was for Huia against [[Ngamatea]] on 12 September where he scored a try and kicked a conversion in an 11–6 win.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Taihape/Rugby Football
| page = 3
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 219
| date = 16 September 1936
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19360916.2.7.7
| access-date = 28 July 2022}}</ref> His final match was for Taihape Māori against a combined [[Tokaanu]]-[[Rātana]] Māori side. He scored a try in a 16–5 win where it was said he played well along with his brother Tommy.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Taihape/Rugby Football
| page = 9
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 79, Issue 230
| date = 29 September 1936
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19360929.2.88
| access-date = 28 July 2022}}</ref>

[[File:Portrait photo from Wanganui playing days.png|thumb|Rangi's older brother Tommy.]]In 1937 on 21 April the Whanganui Chronicle wrote a lengthy piece covering the prospects of various sides in the Whanganui for the coming season. They stated "Tommy Chase, the colourful Māori back from [[Taihape]], is to be in the picture again this year. He is training hard, reports state. Rangi Chase, his brother, the boy who showed promise as an inside back, is in the running too. That pair will do much to stimulate Taihape Rugby this year.<ref>{{cite news
| title = The Rugby World/Season Breaking The Ice
| page = 4
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 80, Issue 93
| date = 21 April 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19370421.2.13
| access-date = 28 July 2022}}</ref> A week later on 24 April in their opening match Huia beat [[Hautapu]] 19 to 18. The Whanganui Chronicle said "Rangi Chase played a brilliant game for the red and blacks and undoubtedly won the match for his side. Hautapu were leading by 18 points to 11 with only a short time to go, but Huia came to light at the finish, thanks to Rangi Chase, and pulled the match out of the fire…". He scored 2 tries, set up another and kicked 2 conversions. His first try came after "a scintillating run… left the opposition standing, and this brilliant player scored under the posts and converted his own try". Then he "once more became the hero of the side when he took a pass from Payne and scored a try in the corner".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Rugby Football/Huia Defeat Hautapu
| page = 3
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 80, Issue 99
| date = 28 April 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19370428.2.6.4
| access-date = 28 July 2022}}</ref> He had played in the centres and did so again against Utiku on 1 May, scoring another try after he "swerved through the defence to score a great try in the corner" which Tommy converted. Despite this they lost 18–8.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Taihape/Rugby Football
| page = 9
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 80, Issue 105
| date = 5 May 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19370505.2.90
| access-date = 28 July 2022}}</ref> On 8 May he kicked a conversion and "went well" in a 13–3 win over Pirates.<ref>{{cite news
| title =Taihape/Rugby Football/Huia Beat Pirates
| page = 3
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 80, Issue 110
| date = 11 May 1936
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19370511.2.8.7
| access-date = 28 July 2022}}</ref> Chase then played in a midweek match on 12 May to celebrate Coronation Day for the Country team against the Town team. He played well once again and scored a try in a 20–9 win.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Taihape/Coronation Day/Rugby Football
| page = 3
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 80, Issue 113
| date = 14 May 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19370514.2.7.3
| access-date = 28 July 2022}}</ref> Three days later Huia beat Hautapu 22 to 0 with it said "this match was a triumph for Rangi and Tommy Chase, who played with perfect understanding and were always a thorn in the side of the opposition. Rangi rose to great heights and played a most prominent part in the Māori team's victory". He was heavily involved in much of the attacking play and "was the hero (or shall we say one of the heroes) of the Huia team, and played brilliantly throughout the match. Accepting a pass from Payne he badly beat the opposition to score under the posts…".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Rugby Football/Huia v. Hautapu
| page = 3
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 80, Issue 116
| date = 18 May 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19370518.2.14
| access-date = 28 July 2022}}</ref> He played in two further matches for Huia against Utiku and Pirates on 22 and 29 May respectively.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Taihape/Rugby Affairs
| page = 3
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 80, Issue 123
| date = 26 May 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19370526.2.6.3
| access-date = 28 July 2022}}</ref>

It was reported on 2 June in the [[Whanganui Chronicle]] that the Taihape Rugby Union secretary informed their weekly meeting that "Rangi Chase had received a tempting offer to play League football in [[Auckland]] and was considering the matter.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Taihape/Rugby Affairs
| page = 3
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 80, Issue 129
| date = 2 June 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19370602.2.6.3
| access-date = 28 July 2022}}</ref> Nonetheless he was still selected for the Taihape team to play Wanganui Metropolitan on 5 June for the Pownall Trophy.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Taihape v. Wanganui
| page = 3
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 80, Issue 129
| date = 2 June 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19370602.2.6.4
| access-date = 28 July 2022}}</ref> Taihape were well beaten 16–3 although Chase "put in some good work at times but seemed to be greatly handicapped by the wet ground".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Match At Taihape/The Play In Review
| page = 5
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 80, Issue 135
| date = 9 June 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19370609.2.28.1
| access-date = 28 July 2022}}</ref> Following the match he was named as the back reserve for the [[Whanganui Rugby Football Union|Whanganui]] side to play [[Manawatu Rugby Union|Manawatu]].<ref>{{cite news
| title = Morning Rugby/Next Wednesday's Match/Wanganui And Manawatu
| page = 5
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 80, Issue 133
| date = 7 June 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19370607.2.16
| access-date = 28 July 2022}}</ref>

==Rugby league==
===Manukau Rugby League===
[[File:Rangi Chase Manukau profile pic.png|250px|left]]Just days later, on 11 June 1937, Rangi and his older brother Tommy left for [[Auckland]]. The [[Wanganui Chronicle]] reporting: "Taihape Rugby in general, and the Huia Football Club in particular, have suffered a severe loss in the departure of Tommy and Rangi Chase for Auckland to play league football. These two prominent footballers left on Friday by car for the Queen City.<ref>{{cite news
| title = General News
| page = 3
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 80, Issue 139
| date = 14 June 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19370614.2.9.4
| access-date = 28 July 2022}}</ref> Unsurprisingly it was Tommy, 6 years Rangi's senior, who reportedly took "his brother Rangi with him".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Rugby Season Ending
| page = 4
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 80, Issue 225
| date = 22 September 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19370922.2.13
| access-date = 28 July 2022}}</ref> They joined the [[Manukau Magpies|Manukau Rugby League club]] which played in the [[Onehunga]] suburb of [[Auckland]]. They debuted for their senior side against [[Richmond Rovers]] on 12 June at [[Carlaw Park]] in the main match at 3pm. Manukau lost a thrilling game 22–18 with Rangi scoring 3 tries and kicking a drop goal. The [[Auckland Star]] reporting that "the particular scintillating star for Manukau was R. Chase second five eighths, who is the most valuable of Manukau's new recruits. He scored eleven of his side's points, and, in addition to being fleet in possession, was sound on defence".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Richmond's Late Rally
| page = 15
| publisher = Auckland Star, Issue LXVIII
| date = 14 June 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370614.2.144.6
| access-date = 28 July 2022}}</ref> The [[New Zealand Herald]] said he was "a fine type of player, Chase did some brilliant work on attack, running strongly and straight and showing ability in making an opening. He also defended strongly". One of his tries came when he ran over Frank Furnell, the Richmond fullback with his "weight carrying him over".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Fine Exhibition/Win For Richmond
| page = 14
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22754
| date = 14 June 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370614.2.162.5
| access-date = 28 July 2022}}</ref> The brothers had their registration with the Manukau club approved at the ARL board meeting on 16 June.<ref>{{cite news
| title = The League Code
| page = 22
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22757
| date = 17 June 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370617.2.189
| access-date = 28 July 2022}}</ref> Following their debut appearances for Manukau the Wanganui Chronicle reported "the two Chase brothers, Tommy and Rangi, of the Huia Club (Taihape) have joined the ranks of league and were playing for Manukau last Saturday. Both were prominent, Rangi particularly. He scored several tries … both have played with a touch of sparkle and have contributed a good deal to the spectacular side of rugby in Taihape".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Chases Go To League
| page = 4
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 80, Issue 141
| date = 16 June 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19370616.2.18
| access-date = 28 July 2022}}</ref>

The following weekend on 19 June several of the senior sides embarked on a 'travel round' to various parts of the North Island. Manukau travelled to Taneatua to play the [[Bay of Plenty rugby league team|Bay of Plenty]]. They won an entertaining match 51–33 with Rangi again scoring 3 tries with Tommy scoring twice. The side also included brilliant Māori player, and [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] representative, [[Jack Hemi]] who scored a try and kicked 9 goals.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Hemi's Great Day
| page = 12
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 145
| date = 21 June 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370621.2.124
| access-date = 29 July 2022}}</ref> A match report said that "Hemi and R. Chase played outstanding football. Both initiated fast attacking movements, and their ability to change direction quickly was a feature of the game". In addition to Hemi, the Manukau side also included New Zealand internationals [[Puti Tipene Watene]], [[Jack Brodrick]], and [[Angus Gault]].<ref>{{cite news
| title = Manukau's Fine Display/Match Against Bay Of Plenty
| page =22
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22762
| date = 23 June 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370623.2.197.2
| access-date = 29 July 2022}}</ref> The Fox Memorial championship then resumed with round 8 matches and Rangi once again found the try line, scoring twice in a 31–11 win over [[North Shore Albions]]. The Star reported that "once more it was demonstrated just what an asset the Chase brothers… are to their side… they were continually in the picture and each registered tries. Quick to dart through the gap, they were a continual thorn in the side of the Shore backs. Both were in fine form, but the beautifully built R. Chase was perhaps a trifle more polished than his more stocky brother. Their fine understanding of each other's play was a feature, and the seemed equally at home at second five-eights and centre when they elected frequently to exchange positions".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Manukau In Form
| page = 12
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 157
| date = 5 July 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370705.2.142
| access-date = 29 July 2022}}</ref> In round 9 Manukau beat [[Marist Saints|Marist Old Boys]] 20–13 with Chase scoring yet again. He and Tommy "were seen in some good allround work".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Disappointing Game/Teams Lack Finish
| page = 14
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22778
| date = 12 July 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370712.2.161.5
| access-date = 29 July 2022}}</ref>

Rangi had been playing in the five-eighths with Tommy, but in their 17 July, round 10 match with [[Newton Rangers]] he moved into the centres to give Wilson, a Bay of Plenty recruit, a chance in the backline.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Manukau's Winning Vein
| page = 15
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 169
| date = 19 July 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370719.2.153.1
| access-date = 29 July 2022}}</ref> Nonetheless "R. and T. Chase played fine football on attack, and took part in most of the scoring movements". In the first half they were involved in "good play, from which two tries were scored", the first to Rangi.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Win For Manukau
| page = 17
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22784
| date = 19 July 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370719.2.191.1
| access-date = 29 July 2022}}</ref>

On 21 July the Auckland Māori (Tamaki) team played the Waikato Māori side for the Waitangi Shield, however both Rangi and Tommy were not available.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Waitangi Shield Game
| page = 15
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 170
| date = 20 July 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370720.2.171
| access-date = 29 July 2022}}</ref> On 24 July Manukau beat [[Ponsonby Ponies|Ponsonby United]] 18–6 in the main match at [[Carlaw Park]]. Rangi played "a fine game at second five-eighths, and did some great all-round work on attack".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Fine All-Round Play/Manukau In Good Form
| page = 14
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22790
| date = 26 July 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370726.2.175.2
| access-date = 29 July 2022}}</ref>

===New Zealand selection and debut and New Zealand Māori===
[[File:NZ team player profile pictures in 1937.png|thumb|right|New Zealand profile pictures including Rangi Chase for first test v Australia.]]Due to his outstanding form in his short time in the game Rangi and Tommy Chase were both selected in the [[New Zealand rugby league team|New Zealand]] trial between the Probables and Possibles. They were paired in the five eighths for the Probable side. The Auckland Star said that "it will be interesting to see how the Chase brothers compare with the [[Richmond Rovers|Richmond]] five-eighths, [[Noel Bickerton|Bickerton]], and [[Wally Tittleton|W. Tittleton]]".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Trial Match/Kangaroo Preparations/Probables v. Possibles
| page = 14
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVII, Issue 176
| date = 27 July 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370727.2.129
| access-date = 29 July 2022}}</ref> The Possibles side won in a "quagmire" 25 to 11. The Star said that "Bickerton and W. Tittleton made a better five-eighths line than the Chase brothers, of Manukau, although for sheer solo ability R. Chase was the best scoring back on the ground" with 3 tries.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Yesterday's Trials/Match In The Mud/Possibles And Probables/Quest For Rep. Players
| page = 20
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 178
| date = 29 July 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370729.2.146
| access-date = 29 July 2022}}</ref> At halftime the Chase brothers were changed over to the Probables side with Rangi making "a good impression".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Representative Trial/Match At Carlaw Park/Conditions Hamper Players
| page = 18
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22793
| date = 29 July 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370729.2.173
| access-date = 29 July 2022}}</ref> The trial was played midweek and on the Saturday following Rangi was back playing for Manukau against Richmond. Interestingly the brothers were once again paired up against Noel Bickerton and Wally Tittleton in their club side. The match was drawn 11–11. Rangi "did some brilliant work on attack. He cut the defence to ribbons and, after three forwards had handled, [[Angus Gault|Gault]] got over in a good position". He was "the pick of the backs".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Manukau Weakened
| page = 14
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22796
| date = 2 August 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370802.2.167.10
| access-date = 29 July 2022}}</ref> He was said to be "the outstanding player… who played a clever attacking game at five-eighths. Many times Chase cut in brilliantly and threw perfect passes to his supports. His splendid all-round play must have impressed the New Zealand selectors, as he is included in the fifteen players to train for the first test on Saturday against the Australians".<ref>{{cite news
| title = A Brilliant Back/R. Chase Outstanding
| page = 18
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22798
| date = 4 August 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370804.2.205.5
| access-date = 29 July 2022}}</ref>

[[File:New Zealand squad to play Australia in 1937.png|left|450px|New Zealand team for the first test v Australia. Chase is in the back row on the left.]] Rangi was picked by New Zealand selectors [[Thomas McClymont]], [[Bert Avery]], and [[Jim Amos (rugby league)|Jim Amos]] for the [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]] series. The New Zealand Herald speculated that "the form of W. Tittleton has not been good lately, and on the day R. Chase may be the five eighths".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Visit Of Australians/Matches In Auckland/New Zealanders To Train
| page = 11
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22796
| date = 2 August 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370802.2.133
| access-date = 29 July 2022}}</ref> [[File:Action from NZ v Australia 7 Aug, 1937.png|thumb|Chase in support of Cec Davison who is scoring for New Zealand in the first test.]]There had been 4 three-quarters chosen, [[Ces Davison]], [[Arthur Kay (rugby league)|Arthur Kay]], [[Brian Riley (rugby league)|Brian Riley]], and Chase. The Herald said that "it is difficult to see Riley, who has only played a few club matches, considered before R. Chase. Riley has played most of his football at five eighths, and during the last few weeks he has now shown anything outstanding as a wing for Ponsonby. Chase has had considerable experience and should make a good wing".<ref>{{cite news
| title = New Zealand Team/The Selection Reviewed
| page = 18
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22798
| date = 4 August 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370804.2.205.5
| access-date = 29 July 2022}}</ref>

Rangi Chase was then selected in the centre position with [[Puti Tipene Watene]] at fullback, [[Ces Davison]] on one wing, and [[Brian Riley (rugby league)|Brian Riley]] on the other.<ref>{{cite news
| title =First Test/Teams For To-Morrow
| page = 9
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 185
| date = 6 August 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370806.2.115
| access-date = 29 July 2022}}</ref> The Herald said Chase's inclusion at centre "has not weakened the back line. Chase has shown excellent club form as a clever attacking back. His immediate opponent will be Beaton, an exceptionally fast player, with more than average scoring powers".<ref>{{cite news
| title = League Test/Game This Afternoon
| page = 17
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22801
| date = 7 August 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370807.2.153
| access-date = 29 July 2022}}</ref>

The first test was played at [[Carlaw Park]] before a crowd of 20,000 and saw Australia run out narrow 12–8 winners after the scores were tied at 6–6 at half time. Chase was involved in the opening try of the match for New Zealand when Davison "transferred to Tittleton, Bickerton, and Chase, and then Bickerton came in again to record the first try after 11 minutes' play".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Australia's Win/New Zealand Beaten/Match At Carlaw Park
| page = 11
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22802
| date = 9 August 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370809.2.140.1
| access-date = 8 August 2022}}</ref> Of Chase after the match it was said that "much of the rugged play fell to R. Chase, whose sound tackling checked McKinnon and Beaton".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Ball Possession/Visitors Superior/Fine Teamwork Shown
| page =11
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22802
| date = 9 August 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370809.2.140.2
| access-date = 8 August 2022}}</ref>

[[File:New Zealand Māori squad to play Australia in 1937.png|thumb|left|New Zealand Māori team to play Australia. Chase is in the back row, second from left.]]Following the test Chase was chosen in the three-quarters of the New Zealand Māori squad to play against Australia on the Wednesday at Carlaw Park.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Match With Maoris/Native Team Chosen
| page = 16
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 187
| date = 9 August 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370809.2.166.2
| access-date = 8 August 2022}}</ref> Despite being named in the three-quarters originally Rangi was moved into the five eighths position alongside his brother Tommy, with Mahima at halfback. [[George Nepia]] was at fullback with [[Jack Hemi]] in the centres.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Second Match/The Kangaroos/Contest With Maori Team
| page = 8
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 189
| date = 11 August 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370811.2.95
| access-date = 8 August 2022}}</ref> Australia elected to make several changes to their first test side and New Zealand Māori ran out comfortable 16–5 winners despite being hampered by  injuries during the match. Rangi Chase scored a try. The New Zealand Herald said that "the two Chase brothers combined nicely behind the scrum, while Broughton and Rata, on the wings, both handicapped by want of the ball, tackled and defended soundly". Chase's try came early in the match after "the home team rushed the ball over the visitors' line and R. Chase scored". Hemi's conversion gave them a 5–0 lead. With the lead at 7–0 the Māori side "had a good chance of scoring but T. Chase knocked it on when his brother had a clear run in".<ref>{{cite news
| title = League Visitors/Surprise By Maoris
| page = 13
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22805
| date = 12 August 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370812.2.129.1
| access-date = 8 August 2022}}</ref>

Chase was going to be replaced by [[Arthur Kay (rugby league)|Arthur Kay]] for the second and final test at Carlaw Park on the following Saturday after recovering from injury. The Herald stated that "the Ponsonby player will need to produce his best form in order to play as well as the Māori centre".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Second Test/Teams For Saturday/Alterations In Home Side
| page = 13
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22805
| date = 12 August 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370812.2.129.2
| access-date = 8 August 2022}}</ref> However [[Brian Riley (rugby league)|Brian Riley]] "ricked his ankle" at training and Chase came back into the side.<ref>{{cite news
| title = League Team/N.Z. Team Changed
| page = 8
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 191
| date = 13 August 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370813.2.94
| access-date = 8 August 2022}}</ref> The match was played on 14 August at Carlaw Park before a crowd of 25,000. New Zealand upset the visitors 16–15 after coming back from a 15–6 deficit at halftime.<ref>{{cite news
| title = New Zealand 16 def. Australia 15
| publisher = Rugby League Project
| url =https://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/matches/new-zealand-vs-australia-1937/game-2/new-zealand-vs-australia/summary.html
| access-date = 8 August 2022}}</ref> It was the Australians turn to be depleted by injuries and they played much of the match two men short after Pearce broke his leg in the first half and Gibbs went off with a rib injury early in the second half. The Auckland Star said that "much of the New Zealand attacking work was ragged, but Davison and Chase were both impressive when in possession".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Win snatched from Kangaroos
| page = 14
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 193
| date = 16 August 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370816.2.143.5
| access-date = 8 August 2022}}</ref> The Herald said that "Chase made the most of his opportunities, although he had not the pace to get clear".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Nepia's Day/A Great Fullback
| page = 14
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22808
| date = 16 August 1937
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370816.2.130.2
| access-date = 8 August 2022}}</ref> This was to be Chase's final match for the season as he missed Manukau's remaining 4 matches along with [[Puti Tipene Watene]], Jack Broughton, and [[Jack Hemi]]. It is possible that he was out of town as many of the Māori players who were not originally from Auckland would often travel home in the off season before returning for the following season.

It was reported in the [[Auckland Star]] in March of [[1938 Auckland Rugby League season|1938]] that Chase would indeed be back to play for [[Manukau Magpies|Manukau]] for the season along with his brother Tommy.<ref>{{cite news
| title = League Football/Matches Next Saturday/Manukau Club's Opening
| page = 21
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 66
| date = 19 March 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380319.2.176
| access-date = 8 August 2022}}</ref>

In a decision which was to have major repercussions for his career while he had been in the Whanganui Province, he had evidently applied for reinstatement into the rugby code. His application was received by the Taihape sub union who forwarded it to the New Zealand Rugby Union who approved it. Despite having his application approved he did not play any rugby games and decided to return to [[Auckland]] to continue playing for [[Manukau Magpies|Manukau]] for the [[1939 Auckland Rugby League season|1939]]. His decision caught the Taihape Rugby Union by surprise.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Rangi Chase reinstated
| page = 6
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 80, Issue 84
| date = 19 March 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19380409.2.16
| access-date = 8 August 2022}}</ref><ref>{{cite news
| title = About Rangi Chase
| page = 4
| publisher = Wanganui Chronicle, Volume 80, Issue 87
| date = 13 April 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19380413.2.9
| access-date = 15 August 2022}}</ref>

He made his first appearance for Manukau in the 1938 season in a round 1 match against [[Newton Rangers]] on 9 April. Manukau lost 19–8 and Chase was forced to leave the field early in the first half with a shoulder injury.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Defeat of Manukau
| page = 20
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23010
| date = 11 April 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380411.2.180.1
| access-date = 8 August 2022}}</ref> The Auckland Star, in reporting the injury said that Chase was living at 16, Windsor Road in Parnell at the time.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Mishaps On Playing Field
| page = 9
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 85
| date = 11 April 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380411.2.93
| access-date = 8 August 2022}}</ref> He only missed one match before returning for Manukau on 23 April in their round 3 match with [[Marist Saints|Marist Old Boys]] which they won 18–4. He was said to have "played soundly" and "a good game at centre" where he "was an ideal link".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Play And Players
| page = 19
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 96
| date = 26 April 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380426.2.166
| access-date = 9 August 2022}}</ref><ref>{{cite news
| title = Manukau Improves
| page = 22
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23022
| date = 27 April 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380427.2.215
| access-date = 9 August 2022}}</ref><ref>{{cite news
| title = Good Second Spell/A Spectacular Try
| page = 18
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23021
| date = 26 April 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380426.2.203
| access-date = 9 August 2022}}</ref> Against [[Mount Albert Lions|Mount Albert United]] the following match he "teamed well" with brother Tommy in a 17–9 win.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Strength In The Backs
| page = 16
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 108
| date = 10 May 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380510.2.105
| access-date = 9 August 2022}}</ref> Then against [[Richmond Rovers]] a week later he scored two tries and set up another for McGuire in an 18–16 win. He was said to be "in fine form at centre and played an outstanding game".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Fortunes Fluctuate/Brilliant Movements
| page = 14
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23038
| date = 16 May 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380516.2.142
| access-date = 9 August 2022}}</ref> His "adroitness in cutting in … gave him two tries which Manukau badly needed".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Best Of The Season
| page = 15
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 114
| date = 17 May 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380517.2.120.1
| access-date = 9 August 2022}}</ref>

Chase was then selected in the [[Auckland rugby league team|Auckland]] team to play against the Rest of the North Island in a [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] trial to look ahead to the selection of the New Zealand team to tour Australia. He was chosen in the centre position by selectors [[Bert Avery]], [[Hec Brisbane]], and A. Renwick, with [[Clarry McNeil]] and [[Brian Riley (rugby league)|Brian Riley]] on the wings and [[Jack Smith (rugby league, New Zealand)|Jack Smith]] at fullback.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Trial Match/Teams For Next Wednesday
| page = 29
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 110
| date = 12 May 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380512.2.204.5
| access-date = 9 August 2022}}</ref> The trial was played on 21 May at Carlaw Park and resulted in a 67–14 thrashing by the Auckland side with Chase scoring twice.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Auckland's Runaway Win
| page = 26
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 116
| date = 19 May 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380519.2.216.1
| access-date = 9 August 2022}}</ref> The Herald said that "Chase played an impressive game at centre, but the test, however was not severe" with the rest of the North Island side being totally outclassed across the field.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Trial Match Played/Auckland's Easy Victory
| page = 11
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23041
| date = 19 May 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380519.2.43
| access-date = 9 August 2022}}</ref>

Chase then was picked in the [[North Island rugby league team|North Island]] team to play the [[South Island rugby league team|South Island]]. At [[Carlaw Park]] on 21 May the North Island side trounced the South Island 55–2 with Chase involved throughout and he scored a try early in the second half which gave the North a 25–2 lead.<ref>{{cite news
| title = North Team Wins/South Outclassed
| page = 13
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23044
| date = 23 May 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380523.2.156.1
| access-date = 9 August 2022}}</ref>

===New Zealand team to tour Australia===
[[File:NZ team photo 1938 to Australia.png|left|350px]] [[File:NZ Team 1938.png|thumb|The New Zealand team just prior to its departure.]][[File:NZ RL team to tour Australia, 1938.png|thumb|The New Zealand team to tour Australia in 1938. Rangi Chase is eighth from the left.]] Following the North Island – [[South Island rugby league team|South Island]] match, Chase was chosen in the [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] team to tour [[Australia]] in June and July. The selectors were [[Hec Brisbane]], [[Jim Amos (rugby league)|Jim Amos]], and [[Thomas McClymont]] (better known as Scotty McClymont). The [[Auckland Star]] said that "Chase and [[Arthur Kay (rugby league)|Kay]] were certain of their places in the centre, the former a smooth-working player".<ref>{{cite news
| title = 22 Players Chosen/Some Surprises
| page = 15
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 119
| date = 23 May 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380523.2.164
| access-date = 9 August 2022}}</ref> The Herald said they were both "clever players on attack and likely to make good in [[Sydney]]".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Forwards Strong/Fast Combination
| page = 11
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23044
| date = 23 May 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380523.2.115
| access-date = 9 August 2022}}</ref> [[File:NZ team photo to visit Australia in 1938.png|thumb|New Zealand team to tour Australia. Chase is in the back on the left next to Jack Hemi.]]In profiling the players the Herald's said "R. Chase (Auckland), centre, is 20 years of age and weighs 13st, 1lb. He first learned his football at Taihape and when 17 years of age represented Wanganui Rugby Union. He can play either at five eights or three-quarters, and last year against Australia played a brilliant game on the wing for New Zealand. Chase has pace and initiative, and may develop into the best attacking back in the team".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Tour Of Australia/Dominion Team's Visit
| page = 23
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23046
| date = 25 May 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380525.2.179
| access-date = 9 August 2022}}</ref>

Prior to the departure for Australia Chase played in one more match for Manukau against [[City Rovers]] on 28 May which Manukau won 23–19. Before departing the Auckland Star commented that "[[Wally Tittleton|W. Tittleton]] and R. Chase have all the ability to make play for their wings. Chase had a great eye for an opening, and is still improving".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Last Man In The Team/Popular Choice
| page = 15
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 126
| date = 31 May 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380531.2.138
| access-date = 9 August 2022}}</ref>

The New Zealand team stayed at the Commercial Hotel in Auckland before departing on 31 May at 4.50pm on board the Niagara. They had earlier attended the funeral of [[Jack Smith (rugby league, New Zealand)|Jack Smith's]] father, W. Smith. Jack was one of the fullbacks selected for the tour along with [[Jack Hemi]], Chase's Manukau teammate, who had come into the side after [[George Nepia]] withdrew for family reasons. The side was farewelled by Sir Ernest Davis, the [[Mayor of Auckland]] at a civic farewell at the [[Auckland Town Hall]]. Selector [[Thomas McClymont]] was accompanying the side as coach.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Rugby League Team/Visit To Australia
| page = 10
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23051
| date = 31 May 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380531.2.109
| access-date = 9 August 2022}}</ref>

Chase played in 7 of the 9 tour matches and was in the centres on each occasion. [[File:NZ v NSW June 1938.png|thumb|Chase tackling Frank Johnstone in the first match of the tour against NSW at the SCG.]]He played in the opening match of the tour against [[New South Wales rugby league team|New South Wales]] at the [[Sydney Cricket Ground]] before a crowd of 28,303.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Custom Match List/NZ Tour of Australia 1938
| publisher = Rugby League Project
| url =https://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/matches/Custom/MzEtNjc2LS0tLS0tLS0tLS0tLS0tLS0tLS0tLQ==
| access-date = 10 August 2022}}</ref><ref>{{cite news
| title = N.Z. Thirteen/Team To Play N.S.W
| page = 8
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 135
| date = 10 June 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380610.2.96
| access-date = 10 August 2022}}</ref> New Zealand lost the match 25–12 after leading by 8 points to 3 at halftime.<ref>{{cite news
| title = New South Wales Firsts 25 def. New Zealand 12
| publisher = Rugby League Project
| url =https://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/matches/kiwis-tour-1938/game-1/nsw-firsts-vs-new-zealand.html
| access-date = 10 August 2022}}</ref>
[[File:Picture of Rangi Chase.png|thumb|left|Picture of Chase published on 13 June in the NZ Herald.]]They played the same opponent 2 days later on Monday, 13 June at the same venue with 18,426 in attendance. This time New Zealand turned the tables with a comfortable 37–18 win with Chase scoring a try. In the first half he was involved in some attacking play when "in a loose movement McNeight got the ball to Chase, who threw it on to Smith, and the last named scored in the corner" with Hemi's conversion narrowing the score to 8–7 in NSW's favour. A short time later "McNeight and McLeod … dribbled through, and Chase gathering it, beat Conlon to score again", the conversion gave New Zealand 12–8 lead.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Rough Play In Big Rugby League Match/N.Z. v. N.S.W.
| page = 8
| publisher = The Newcastle Sun (NSW: 1918 – 1954)
| date = 13 June 1938
| url = http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article166709174
| access-date = 10 August 2022}}</ref><ref>{{cite news
| title = Surprise In League Game
| page = 13
| publisher = The Sun (Sydney, NSW: 1910–1954)
| date = 13 June 1938
| url = http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article231035456
| access-date = 10 August 2022}}</ref> [[The Queensland Times]] reported that "a feature of the game was the excellent tackling of W. Tittleton and R. Chase, the "Kiwi" centres. These two players broke up the New South Wales attack in no uncertain fashion, and gave F. Hyde and R. Roser, the New South Wales centre three-quarters, a torrid time".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Match Of Thrills/Kiwi Rout N.S.W.
| page = 10
| publisher = Queensland Times (Ipswich, Qld.: 1909 – 1954)
| date = 14 June 1938
| url = http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article115610814
| access-date = 10 August 2022}}</ref>

[[File:Portrait pic in Brisbane telegraph.png|thumb|Picture of Chase in the Brisbane Telegraph on 20 June 1938.]]Chase was rested from the next match which New Zealand won 23–2 against North Coast on 15 June at Lismore Recreation Ground in Lismore.<ref>{{cite news
| title = North Coast 2 lost to New Zealand 23
| publisher = Rugby League Project
| url = http://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/matches/kiwis-tour-1938/game-3/north-coast-vs-new-zealand.html
| access-date = 10 August 2022}}</ref> He played in the fourth tour match against [[Queensland rugby league team|Queensland]] at the [[Brisbane Cricket Ground]] on 18 June.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Taking No Ricks/Match With Queensland
| page = 10
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 142
| date = 18 June 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380618.2.78.4
| access-date = 11 August 2022}}</ref> New Zealand was outclassed and lost 31 to 11. Prior to the match the Courier Mail in Brisbane had written of Chase: "The Kiwis possess two grand centres in W. Tittleton (Auckland) and R. Chase, the 20 year old Māori. These players broke up the New South Wales attack on Monday, and gave Hyde and Roser, the New South Wales centres a torrid time. Chase represented rugby union at 17 years of age. He is very versatile, and has played with distinction at standoff half, centre, and wing. He is one of the most dangerous attacking backs in the side".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Backs Speedy, Clever
| page = 12
| publisher = The Courier-Mail (Brisbane, Qld. : 1933–1954)
| date = 17 June 1938
| url = http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article41002984
| access-date = 10 August 2022}}</ref> Queensland dominated the scrums and as a result the New Zealand backs received little ball. Wally Tittleton "made a few openings and was backed up by Chase, but lack of support ruined his efforts when tries might have resulted".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Heavy Defeat/Rugby League Team/Dominion Side Outclassed
| page = 11
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23068
| date = 20 June 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380620.2.93
| access-date = 11 August 2022}}</ref> The Telegraph newspaper in Brisbane said "occasionally we saw flashed of brilliance from Kiwi winger Smith, and Chase in the centre. But their opportunities were so rare that it would be difficult to judge their real ability".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Kiwis Gave Maroons Too Much Latitude/Weak Tackling Marred Defensive Play
| page = 20
| publisher = The Telegraph (Brisbane, Qld. : 1872 – 1947)
| date = 20 June 1938
| url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article183486975
| access-date = 11 August 2022}}</ref> The Referee newspaper in Sydney echoed those words saying "Tittleton and Chase in the centre seldom got an even break in attack, due primarily to the inability of five eighth [[Walter Brimble|W. P. Brimble]] to get them on the run". At one stage Chase "slipped a Melrose tackle in the first half to get through the Maroons' defence, but he did not keep the form going".<ref>{{cite news
| title = New Zealanders Outplayed By Fiery Forwards/Hemi's Phenomenal Goal Kicking
| page = 14
| publisher = NEW ZEALANDERS OUTPLAYED BY FIERY FORWARDS (23 June 1938). Referee (Sydney, NSW : 1886 – 1939)
| date = 23 June 1938
| url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article127497581
| access-date = 11 August 2022}}</ref>

[[File:Rangi Chase and Ray Brown at the Brisbane train station.png|left|350px]]Against Toowoomba Chase was again in the centres though this time he was paired with [[Jack Smith (rugby league, New Zealand)|Jack Smith]] for the 22 June match. New Zealand won by the narrow margin of 12–11 and did not impress before a crowd of 2,500 at the Athletic Ground.<ref>{{cite news
| title =Close Call For Kiwis
| page = 12
| publisher = The Courier-Mail (Brisbane, Qld. : 1933 – 1954)
| date = 23 June 1938
| url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article40995224
| access-date = 11 August 2022}}</ref> New Zealand then played Queensland once again at the Brisbane Cricket Ground and were defeated 21–12 after leading 12–5 at halftime. Chase partnered [[Jack Hemi]] in the centres this time. Hemi had been in magnificent form in the tour but had been played at fullback.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Queensland Firsts 21 def. New Zealand 12
| publisher = Rugby League Project
| url =http://rugbyleagueproject.org/matches/30653
| access-date = 11 August 2022}}</ref> After the match Chase was pictured with [[Ray Brown (New Zealand)|Ray Brown]] at Central Station in Brisbane where they were about to depart on the Wallangarra mail train to play Tamworth.<ref>{{cite news
| title = The Kiwis Leave Brisbane
| page = 23
| publisher = The Telegraph (Brisbane, Qld. : 1872 – 1947)
| date = 28 June 1938
| url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article183478779
| access-date = 11 August 2022}}</ref> Chase was rested from the match with his next appearance against Newcastle on 2 July at the Newcastle Sports Ground before 5,000 spectators. New Zealand played well in a 30–19 win. Their final try had the crowd cheering for "several minutes" with [[Wilfred Brimble]] picking the ball up in his own twenty five and passing it "to Tittleton, who passed to Chase. [[Wilfred Brimble|W. Brimble]] took it again and the ball then went to [[Walter Brimble|W. P. Brimble]] and on to [[John Anderson (rugby league)|John Anderson]], Tittleton and [[Jack McLeod (rugby league)|Jack McLeod]], for the last-named to score and [[Jack Hemi|Hemi]] to add the goal. It was league football at its best".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Brilliant Play/Kiwis On Tour
| page = 17
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23080
| date = 4 July 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380704.2.187
| access-date = 11 August 2022}}</ref> The final match of the tour was against [[City New South Wales rugby league team|Sydney]] at the [[Sydney Cricket Ground]] on 6 July. New Zealand trailed 11–5 at halftime before coming back to draw the match 19–19 before a crowd of 5,000. Though they could have won if not for a late Sydney try. In the first half "there was a fine handling burst in which [[Wilfred Brimble|Brimble]], Hemi, Kay and Chase took part to carry the game into the home 25. From there New Zealand rushed the ball over the line and McLeod scored a few feet from touch in goal" to give New Zealand a 3–0 lead. The match see-sawed from there with New Zealand attacking when the game ended. Chase had been partnered with yet another centre in the match, playing alongside [[Arthur Kay (rugby league)|Arthur Kay]]<ref>{{cite news
| title = Sydney Snatches A Draw, 19-All
| page = 32
| publisher = The Sun (Sydney, NSW : 1910 – 1954)
| date = 6 July 1938
| url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article229141066
| access-date = 11 August 2022}}</ref>

When the New Zealand side returned they played a match against [[Auckland rugby league team|Auckland]] at [[Carlaw Park]] on 16 July.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Kiwis Parade/Auckland Game
| page = 15
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 165
| date = 15 July 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380715.2.177
| access-date = 11 August 2022}}</ref> Auckland won the match 21–13 before 15,000 spectators with Chase scoring a try. His brother Tommy played for Auckland and put in a brilliant performance. Rangi Chase himself "disclosed a nice eye for an opening and wonderful ability to take the gap or change the trend of play".<ref>{{cite news
| title = League Thrill/Great Recovery/Auckland On Top
| page = 12
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 167
| date = 18 July 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380718.2.140
| access-date = 11 August 2022}}</ref> In the first half he "made one brilliant cut through … which led to a try". He had received a pass from [[Arthur Kay (rugby league)|Arthur Kay]] before he "turned smartly infield and beat three Auckland backs before he passed to McLeod, who scored between the posts". Chase's try came when [[Wally Tittleton]] "raced well into Auckland territory before passing to Kay" and Chase then scored to give New Zealand a 13–3 lead before a big comeback by Auckland turned the result.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Dominion Team/Auckland's Victory
| page = 13
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23092

| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380718.2.158.1
| access-date = 11 August 2022}}</ref>

Chase then played 5 further matches for [[Manukau Magpies|Manukau]] before the season end as well as his first, and only representative match for [[Auckland rugby league team|Auckland]]. The first of the matches for Manukau was against [[City Rovers]] in round 14 of the Fox Memorial championship. Manukau won 18–4. Both the "Chase brothers got few chances, but both played well".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Hemi's Good Form/City Defeated
| page = 14
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23098
| date = 25 July 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380725.2.147
| access-date = 11 August 2022}}</ref> Manukau then drew with [[Richmond Rovers]] 6–6 on 30 July with Rangi and Tommy both "prominent on defence".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Richmond Unlucky/Fine Goal-Kicking

| date = 1 August 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380801.2.152
| access-date = 12 August 2022}}</ref> Rangi then hit top form in the round 16 win over [[Mount Albert Lions|Mount Albert United]] by 31 points to 5. The Herald said that "R. Chase was in brilliant form", and that "he was the best back on the ground… at centre three-quarter… he changed direction with ease and used his speed in advantage" and scored 3 of Manukau's 7 tries.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Splendid Display/Mount Albert Beaten
| page = 14
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23110
| date = 8 August 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380808.2.174
| access-date = 12 August 2022}}</ref> In a later article on the match the Herald said that "perfect understanding between [Peter] Mahima and T. Chase gave R. Chase a chance to show his brilliance on attack. The clever manner in which R. Chase stepped inside a tackle made tries look easy, although there was a lot of merit in his three tries".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Senior Championship/Two Outstanding Players
| page = 21
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23112
| date = 10 August 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380810.2.223

Chase was attractive, and his forceful method of going for the line when in possession was in keeping with the best traditions of league back play".<ref>{{cite news
| title = League Code/Canterbury Visit/Bright Type Of Play/Club Championship Open
| page = 14
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 192
| date = 16 August 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380816.2.160
| access-date = 12 August 2022}}</ref> His first try came after "poor passing by the Canterbury rearguard" to narrow the score to 5–3 to Canterbury. He scored later when brother Tommy "broke through and sent on to [Rangi] who forced his way past Boniface and scored wide out". [[Jack Hemi|Jack Hemi's]] conversion gave Auckland a 13–12 lead. The Herald said he was "the best back on the ground… [and] his ability to change direction often beat the Ponsonby defence".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Fast Display/Manukau Too Strong

| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380822.2.163
| access-date = 12 August 2022}}</ref> The Auckland Star agreed, saying Manukau's "outstanding back on the day was R. Chase, in the centre, who was extremely dangerous in attack".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Marist Win

| date = 23 August 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380823.2.172
| access-date = 12 August 2022}}</ref> In a season summary in the New Zealand Herald it was said that "a lot of credit must go to J. Hemi and R. Chase, at centre three-quarter, the latter being the most-improved player in the code".<ref>{{cite news

| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23124
| date = 24 August 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380824.2.199.4
| access-date = 12 August 2022}}</ref> Chase did not play in any of Manukau's Roope Rooster or Phelan Shield knockout matches in September. It was reported that he was "out of Auckland".<ref>{{cite news
| title = Ponsonby's Defeated/Phelan Shield Final
| page = 17
| publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 228
| date = 27 September 1938
| url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380927.2.142.7
| access-date = 12 August 2022}}</ref> [[File:Manukau v Eastern Suburbs in 1938 advertisement.png|thumb|Advertisement for the Manukau - Eastern Suburbs game with Chase's name featured.]]He did return however in time for Manukau's match against the visiting [[Sydney Roosters|Eastern Suburbs]] team from [[Sydney]]. Eastern Suburbs had finished runner up in the [[1938 NSWRFL season|NSWRFL]] competition.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Sydney Team's Visit/Match Against Manukau
| page = 9
| publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23153

Chase was heavily involved in the attack for the losers but many of the movements he was involved in ended in nothing.<ref>{{cite news
